1:
"Then shall the kingdom of heaven be likened unto ten virgins." The parable opens the discourse on the great day, and the witnesses divide sharply over the meaning of its central image, the oil. The Antiochene Chrysostom reads the oil as "charity, alms, and every aid rendered to the needy," the lamps as "the gifts of virginity"; the foolish are foolish because, "after having gone through the greater toil" of chastity, "they lost all for the sake of a less," lacking works of mercy. The Latin Augustine reads it inwardly as "joy" (Psalm 45:7), the gladness of a conscience "inwardly pleasing to God," so that he "whose joy springs not from this... has no oil with him," seeking only "the praises of men" (Galatians 6:4).

The other fathers multiply the senses. Jerome takes the oil as "the ornament of good works" added to faith, and, against a merely literal reading "of virgins," refers the parable "not to virgins only, but to the whole human race," the ten standing for the five senses doubled. The Alexandrian Origen reads the oil as "the word of teaching," the lamps as the natural faculties, and the foolish as those who profess faith "but prepare themselves not by good works." Gregory the Great takes the kingdom as "the present Church," wise mixed with foolish as in the net (Matthew 13:41), all having lamps but not all oil.

The Puritan Henry and the grammatical-historical commentators keep to the marriage custom and the moral. Henry expounds the bridegroom as Christ (Psalm 45), the virgins as professors, the lamps as the light of profession the children of light must hold forth (Philippians 2:15); the wise have "a good principle within," the grace which is "the oil" derived from Christ "by the golden pipes of the ordinances" (Zechariah 4:2; John 1:16). Lightfoot supplies the Jewish background — the ten as the customary minimum for a wedding company. The Reformed Calvin keeps the single point: habitual readiness for a coming whose hour is unknown. So the witnesses agree the lamp is profession and the oil the inward reality, while parting over whether that reality is charity, joy, good works, or sound doctrine.
2:
"And five of them were wise, and five were foolish." The witnesses read the equal division as a solemn distinction within the visible Church, and several note its charitable balance. The Puritan Henry makes the verse the occasion for his fullest reflection on the two characters: "those of the same profession and denomination among men may yet be of characters vastly different in the sight of God. Sincere Christians are the wise virgins, and hypocrites the foolish." He adds that "true religion is true wisdom; sin is folly, but especially the sin of hypocrisy, for those are the greatest fools that are wise in their own conceit." On the even number he cites Archbishop Tillotson's "charitable decorum," that Christ "would teach us to hope the best concerning those that profess religion" — though "in judging of ourselves we ought to remember that the gate is strait, and few find it."

The grammatical-historical commentators (Lightfoot, Barnes, Ellicott) keep to the framework of the wedding company, noting that wisdom and folly are here measured wholly by the single matter of provision: the wise foresaw the possible delay and prepared, the foolish did not. The Reformed Calvin reads the division as the abiding truth that profession is no proof of grace, the same warning the whole parable presses.

The witnesses agree that the difference between wise and foolish is not in the outward calling — both are virgins, both carry lamps, both go forth to meet the bridegroom — but in the inward provision that alone can sustain the profession to the end. As Gill notes, the wisdom and folly lie "in the affairs of their souls," the one sort having grace within, the other only the show of it. So the verse sets the parable's whole burden in a single contrast: an identical profession, and beneath it two utterly different states, which only the bridegroom's coming will at last reveal. Gill notes that the wise and foolish alike bear the name of virgins and carry lamps, the difference lying not in the profession but in the secret state behind it, which the bridegroom's coming alone will lay bare; so the verse warns every professor to ask which of the two companies he truly belongs to.
3:
"They that were foolish took their lamps, and took no oil with them." The witnesses fix on this as the precise mark of the hypocrite: a profession without the inward principle to sustain it. The Puritan Henry diagnoses the folly in two parts. First, "they have no principle within": "a lamp of profession in their hands, but not in their hearts that stock of sound knowledge, rooted dispositions, and settled resolutions" needful "through the services and trials of the present state" — like "the seed on the stony ground that wanted root." Second, "they have no prospect of, nor make provision for, what is to come": they "took lamps for a present show, but not oil for after use," all their care being "to recommend themselves to their neighbours, not to approve themselves to Christ."

The grammatical-historical commentators (Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson) and JFB keep to the narrative point: the foolish had oil enough to light the lamp "for the present, to make a show," but no reserve "if the bridegroom tarried." The flaw was not in the lamp but in the want of a supply against delay — the very contingency the parable is about to introduce.

So the witnesses read the verse as the quiet exposure of a religion calculated only for the present moment. The foolish are not those who have no lamp, nor those whose lamp will not light; they are those who never reckoned with the long waiting and the searching test that the bridegroom's tarrying would bring. As Henry puts it, "this incogitancy is the ruin of many professors" — a profession that shines for the eyes of men but has laid in nothing for the hour when Christ himself shall come. Gill and the others agree that the want of oil is the want of true and saving grace, which alone can carry the profession through to the end. The witnesses agree that the foolish are condemned not for having no lamp but for having no reserve, a religion fitted only for the calm of the present and wholly unprovided for the long delay and the searching test that the bridegroom's tarrying was certain to bring.
4:
"But the wise took oil in their vessels with their lamps." The witnesses read the wisdom of the prudent virgins as the laying up of an inward store against the coming trial. The Puritan Henry expounds the figure with care: "they had a good principle within, which would maintain and keep up their profession." The heart, he says, "is the vessel, which it is our wisdom to get furnished; for out of a good treasure there, good things must be brought; but if that root be rottenness, the blossom will be dust." And "grace is the oil which we must have in this vessel" — as in the tabernacle "there was constant provision made of oil for the light" (Exodus 35:14) — for our light "cannot shine before men in good works... unless there be a fixed active principle in the heart, of faith in Christ, and love to God."

Henry presses the foresight the act implies: "they took oil in their vessels upon supposition that perhaps the bridegroom might tarry. In looking forward it is good to prepare for the worst, to lay in for a long siege." The grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson and the Wesleyan Wesley keep to the same plain sense: the wise carried a reserve, so that whatever the delay, their lamps would not fail.

So the verse, in the witnesses' reading, is the positive counterpart to the folly of the foolish. The wisdom of the wise is not a brighter lamp or a louder profession but a hidden provision — grace treasured in the heart, drawn (as Henry adds) from "Jesus Christ, the great and good Olive, by the golden pipes of the ordinances" (Zechariah 4:2; John 1:16). The lamp is what men see; the oil in the vessel is what God sees and what alone will answer at midnight. Gill notes that this inward supply is the grace of the Spirit, which the wise possessed in reality and not in show. So the parable's whole contrast is gathered here: the lamp is the profession every virgin shares, but the oil in the vessel is the hidden grace that distinguishes the wise, and it is that unseen provision, not the visible flame, on which the bridegroom's welcome will at last depend.
5:
"While the bridegroom tarried, they all slumbered and slept." The witnesses ponder both the delay and the universal drowsiness, and divide over what the sleeping signifies. The Latin Jerome and the fathers generally read it of death: "they slumbered, i.e. they were dead, and then follows, and slept, because they were to be afterwards wakened," the tarrying showing "that no little time intervened between the Lord's first and second coming." Jerome adds the Jewish and apostolic tradition that Christ would come "at midnight," as the destroyer passed over Egypt (Exodus 12:29), whence the Easter vigil was kept till midnight (Psalm 119:62).

The Puritan Henry prefers a different reading — not death but spiritual remissness — while noting the patristic view. "Christ, as to us, seems to tarry, and yet really does not" (Habakkuk 2:3); "there is good reason for the Bridegroom's tarrying," that "the elect must all be called in, God's patience manifested, and the saints' patience tried." Of the sleep he distinguishes degrees: "the wise virgins slumbered, and the foolish slept... however, they were both faulty"; the wise "kept their lamps burning, but did not keep themselves awake." He draws the warning that "too many good Christians, when long in profession, grow remiss," and that "one degree of carelessness makes way for another — they slumbered, and then they slept... dread the beginning of spiritual decays."

The Reformed Calvin (whom Henry cites) and the grammatical-historical voices take the tarrying as the great fact the parable turns on: the long interval that tests profession. The Wesleyan Wesley keeps the plain admonition to watchfulness. So the witnesses leave a real difference — whether the sleep is the death of all before the resurrection, or the spiritual drowsiness that creeps over even waiting saints — yet agree that the bridegroom's delay is the appointed trial, and that the all-too-common slumber, even of the wise, is the parable's standing rebuke to a faith grown careless with waiting. Gill adds that the universal drowsiness shows how, in the long interval before Christ comes, even the watchful are apt to abate their first zeal, so that the parable rebukes not the foolish only but every Christian whose love has grown cold with waiting.
6:
"And at midnight there was a cry made, Behold, the bridegroom cometh; go ye out to meet him." The witnesses dwell on the suddenness of the coming and the summons it brings. The Puritan Henry draws out four notes. First, "though Christ tarry long, he will come at last; though he seem slow, he is sure" — "the vision is for an appointed time, and at the end it shall speak, and not lie" (Habakkuk 2:3). Second, "Christ's coming will be at our midnight, when we least look for him, and are most disposed to take our rest"; as "at midnight the first-born of Egypt were destroyed and Israel delivered" (Exodus 12:29), so "death often comes when least expected — the soul is required this night" (Luke 12:20). Third, "when Christ comes, we must go forth to meet him," going out "of the body, out of the world, with affections suitable." Fourth, "the notice given will be awakening; there was a cry made" — for "his second coming will be with the observation of all the world; every eye shall see him," descending "with a shout."

The Alexandrian Origen reads the midnight cry as the voice "of the Angels, desiring to arouse all men," sounding "in the senses of all that sleep"; all hear and arise, "but all were not able to trim their lamps fitly." The Latin Jerome and the fathers take the midnight as the hour of deepest security, "when all think themselves secure," the coming proclaimed "by the shout of Angels and the trumpets of the Powers."

The grammatical-historical, Reformed and Wesleyan voices keep to the plain force: the long delay ends in an instant, with a cry that gives no time to provide what was not already in hand. So the verse, across the schools, is the hinge of the parable — the unexpected, irreversible arrival that turns the whole question upon whether the oil was laid in beforehand, for the time of buying is now past. Gill notes that the cry comes precisely when it is least expected and gives no leisure to provide what was not already laid in, so that the suddenness of the summons is itself the parable's argument for a readiness kept always in hand.
7:
"Then all those virgins arose, and trimmed their lamps." The witnesses read the universal rousing as the moment that exposes the difference long hidden beneath a common profession. The Puritan Henry distinguishes what the same act meant in each. In the wise it "bespeaks an actual preparation for the Bridegroom's coming," for "even those that are best prepared for death have, upon the immediate arrests of it, work to do, to get themselves actually ready, that they may be found in peace (2 Peter 3:14), found doing (Matthew 24:46), and not found naked (2 Corinthians 5:3)." When the day approaches, he says, "we must address ourselves to our dying work with all seriousness, renewing our repentance, our consent to the covenant, our farewells to the world."

But in the foolish the same trimming "denotes a vain confidence, and conceit of the goodness of their state." Henry presses the point: "even counterfeit graces will serve a man to make a show of when he comes to die, as well as they have done all his life long; the hypocrite's hopes blaze when they are just expiring, like a lightening before death." The trimming of the foolish lamps is a last flare of profession that has no reserve to feed it.

The grammatical-historical and Wesleyan voices keep to the narrative: all alike snuffed and dressed their lamps and prepared to receive the bridegroom, so that for a moment the two companies looked the same. The verse thus holds the parable at its point of suspense — every virgin awake, every lamp trimmed, the wise and the foolish outwardly indistinguishable — until the next moment, when the foolish lamps gutter for want of oil and the long-concealed difference comes at last into the open. As Gill notes, the act common to both only sets in sharper relief the supply present in the one and absent in the other. Gill adds that the act common to both companies only sets in sharper relief the supply present in the one and absent in the other, so that the moment of trimming, far from equalising them, is the very point at which their long-hidden difference begins to appear.
8:
"And the foolish said unto the wise, Give us of your oil; for our lamps are gone out." The witnesses read this as the hypocrite's late and vain discovery of his want. The Puritan Henry draws out the misery of the foolish state. "Their lamps are gone out" — "the lamps of hypocrites often go out in this life, when they who have begun in the spirit end in the flesh" (2 Peter 2:20); and even those who keep up their credit to the last find it fail "when God taketh away the soul" (Job 27:8). "An external profession well humoured may carry a man far, but it will not carry him through; it may light him along this world, but the damps of the valley of the shadow of death will put it out."

On the request "Give us of your oil," Henry hears the turning of the tables: "the day is coming when carnal hypocrites would gladly be found in the condition of true Christians. Those who now hate the strictness of religion will, at death and judgment, wish for the solid comforts of it; those who care not to live the life, yet would die the death, of the righteous." Yet the request "comes too late: God would have given them oil, had they asked in time; but there is no buying when the market is over, no bidding when the inch of candle is dropped."

The grammatical-historical, Reformed and Wesleyan voices keep the plain sense: the foolish, awakened, find their lamps failing and have no resource but to beg of others what cannot be borrowed. So the verse exposes the fatal flaw of a borrowed or merely outward religion. The oil that was never laid in cannot be supplied at the last; and the cry "our lamps are gone out" is the cry of those who built only for the present and now meet the very delay and trial they never reckoned with. Gill notes that the failing lamp is the failing of all false profession in the hour of extremity. Gill observes that the failing lamp is the failure of all false profession in the hour of extremity, when the oil that was never laid in cannot be supplied at the last, and the cry of the foolish is the cry of those who built only for the present and now meet the very trial they never reckoned with.
9:
"Not so; lest there be not enough for us and you: but go ye rather to them that sell, and buy for yourselves." The witnesses weigh both the refusal and its reason, and they read in it a sober doctrine of personal grace. The Latin Jerome insists the wise "do not answer thus out of covetousness, but out of fear," for "each man shall receive the recompense of his own works, and the virtues of one cannot atone for the vices of another in the day of judgment." The oil, he adds, "is sold, and at a high cost, nor is it to be got without much toil."

The Puritan Henry draws the same lesson and turns it against Rome. "Those that would be saved must have grace of their own. Though we have benefit by the communion of saints... yet our own sanctification is indispensably necessary to our own salvation"; "every man shall give account of himself... he cannot get another to muster for him in that day." And: "those that have most grace have none to spare; all we have is little enough for ourselves to appear before God. The church of Rome, which dreams of works of supererogation and the imputation of the righteousness of saints, forgets that it was the wisdom of the wise virgins to understand that they had but oil enough for themselves, and none for others."

The Antiochene Chrysostom reads it as teaching that "none of us shall be able in that day to stand forth as patron of those who are betrayed by their own works, not because he will not, but because he cannot"; the poor are the merchants from whom oil must be bought, "but the poor are not there, but here," so "we must store up oil here." The Reformed Calvin and the grammatical voices keep the plain point: grace is not transferable, and the time to obtain it is now. So the refusal, far from unkindness, states the parable's hardest truth — that at the judgment no borrowed or imputed holiness of another can supply what a man has failed to lay in for himself.
10:
"And while they went to buy, the bridegroom came; and they that were ready went in with him to the marriage: and the door was shut." The witnesses read this as the irreversible close of the day of grace. The Antiochene Chrysostom takes the words "while they went to buy" to show "that even if we should become merciful after death, it will avail us nothing to escape punishment, as it was no profit to the rich man that he became merciful... about those who belonged to him" (Luke 16). The time for laying in oil is now; once the bridegroom comes, the opportunity is gone.

The Latin Jerome presses the shut door: "because the season for buying was now past, and the day of judgment coming on, there was no room for penitence; they must not now lay up new works, but give an account of the old. After the day of judgment there is no more opportunity for good works or for righteousness, and therefore it follows, And the door was shut." The Latin Augustine reads the entering in as the pure soul "united to the pure and perfect Word of God," after which "all entrance into the kingdom is closed; after the judgment there is no more place for prayers or merit."

The grammatical-historical and Reformed voices keep the plain and solemn sense: the ready went in, the unready were shut out, and the door once shut is shut for ever. So the witnesses make the verse the parable's point of no return. The whole suspense of the waiting and the trimming resolves in a single instant: those who had the oil "went in with him to the marriage," and the door that admitted them excluded all the rest. Gill and the others note the finality — the shut door is the fixed gulf, the end of probation, the moment after which no provision can be made and no entrance gained. Gill and the others note the finality of the shut door: it is the fixed gulf, the end of probation, the moment after which no provision can be made and no entrance gained, so that the whole suspense of the waiting resolves in a single irreversible instant.
11:
"Afterward came also the other virgins, saying, Lord, Lord, open to us." The witnesses hear in this late and desperate cry the hopeless petition of those who come when the day of grace is past. The grammatical-historical commentators note the doubled "Lord, Lord," and connect it to Christ's warning that "not every one that saith unto me, Lord, Lord, shall enter into the kingdom" (Matthew 7:21-22). The foolish at last call upon the bridegroom by the name they had slighted in their lives, but the calling now cannot open what their negligence has shut.

The Gloss, cited among the fathers, observes the pathos of the title: "grief at their exclusion extorts from them a repetition of this title of Lord; they call not Him Father, whose mercy they despised in their lifetime." The relation of mercy they would not have while it was offered, they cannot claim now that judgment has come; they can only appeal to his lordship, not to his fatherhood.

The witnesses, across the schools, read the verse as the answer of the shut door to the late petitioner. The foolish virgins are not refused admission for want of asking — they ask earnestly and repeatedly — but for want of having asked in time, and of having the oil that asking now cannot supply. Their cry is sincere, urgent, and unavailing, the very picture of those who, as Henry observed on the preceding verses, "will see their need of grace hereafter, when it should save them, who will not see their need of grace now, when it should sanctify them." Gill and Barnes note that the repeated "Lord, Lord" is the cry of a faith that confesses too late, when the season of mercy is over and only the season of reckoning remains. Gill and Barnes note that the repeated 'Lord, Lord' is the cry of a faith that confesses too late, when the season of mercy is over and only the season of reckoning remains, so that the earnestness of the petition cannot make up for the negligence that let the season pass. The witnesses note that the foolish are not refused for want of asking — they ask earnestly and repeatedly — but for want of having asked in time, and of having the oil that no late asking can now supply.
12:
"But he answered and said, Verily I say unto you, I know you not." The witnesses read this as the most terrible word of the parable, the bridegroom's disowning of those who had no oil. The Latin Jerome explains both the confession and the rejection: "their worthy confession calling Him, Lord, Lord, is a mark of faith; but what avails it to confess with the mouth Him whom you deny with your works? For the Lord knoweth them that are his (2 Timothy 2:19), and he that knoweth not shall not be known"; though they be "virgins in purity of body or in confession of the true faith, yet forasmuch as they have no oil, they are unknown by the bridegroom."

The grammatical-historical commentators take "I know you not" not as ignorance but as non-acknowledgement — the judicial disowning of those whom Christ does not own as his own. To be "known" of God is to be approved and acknowledged; to be told "I know you not" is to be cast off from that saving recognition, however confident the profession.

Cyril of Jerusalem turns the verse to exhortation, praying that none enrolled as a candidate for the kingdom may hear "Friend, how camest thou in hither" (Matthew 22:12) or this "I know you not," but rather "wash your robes in repentance, that when called to the bride-chamber ye may be found clean." So the witnesses make the verse the parable's verdict: the bridegroom's recognition is the whole of salvation, and it rests not on the lamp of profession nor even on virgin purity, but on the oil — the inward reality, whatever its precise name — without which the most earnest "Lord, Lord" meets only the closed and final "I know you not." Gill notes that this disowning is the same Christ pronounces on the workers of iniquity who prophesied in his name (Matthew 7:23). Gill notes that this disowning is the same sentence Christ pronounces on the workers of iniquity who prophesied in his name (Matthew 7:23), so that the parable's terror is precisely this, that purity of profession and confidence of address avail nothing where the bridegroom does not own the soul as his own.
13:
"Watch therefore, for ye know neither the day nor the hour wherein the Son of man cometh." The witnesses take this as the parable's express moral, the application that gathers up the whole. The Latin Jerome states it directly: Christ "means that all that has been said points to this, namely, that seeing we know not the day of judgment, we should be careful in providing the light of good works." The unknown hour is the reason for the unceasing readiness.

The Latin Augustine adds a searching personal turn: "we know the day and the hour neither of that future time when the Bridegroom will come, nor of our own falling asleep; if then we be prepared for this latter, we shall also be prepared when that voice shall sound which shall arouse us all." The watchfulness commanded is therefore not anxiety about dates but a constant fitness, equally ready for death and for the coming.

The grammatical-historical and Wesleyan voices keep the plain command: the ignorance of the time is given precisely to keep us always prepared. Cyril of Jerusalem joins the verse to his exhortation to keep the robes clean against the bridegroom's summons. So the witnesses agree the parable does not end in mere warning but in a charge: because the oil cannot be bought at the last, and the door once shut is shut for ever, and the hour is wholly hidden, the only safety is to "watch" — that is, to keep the inward provision of grace always laid in, the lamp always trimmed, the heart always fit to meet the Lord whenever he comes. Gill notes that this watchfulness is not sleepless gazing but the habitual readiness of a soul furnished with grace, prepared at every moment for a coming whose time none can foresee. Gill adds that this watchfulness is not sleepless gazing but the habitual readiness of a soul furnished with grace, prepared at every moment for a coming whose time none can foresee, so that the parable ends not in fear but in the steady charge to keep the oil always in store.
14:
"For the kingdom of heaven is as a man travelling into a far country, who called his own servants, and delivered unto them his goods." The witnesses agree that the travelling man is Christ ascending, and they expound the entrusted goods as gifts to be used. The Latin Gregory the Great reads the journey as the Redeemer ascending "in that flesh which He had taken upon Him," the flesh's proper home being earth, so that it "travels into a foreign country when placed by the Redeemer in heaven." The Alexandrian Origen guards the point doctrinally: Christ "travels not according to His divine nature" — for as the Only-Begotten He says "Lo, I am with you always" (Matthew 28:20) — "but according to the dispensation of the flesh."

On the goods, the fathers read graded gifts. Gregory takes the five talents as "the gift of the five senses, the knowledge of things without," the two as "understanding and action," the one as "understanding only"; to hide a talent in the earth "is to devote the ability we have received to worldly business." Jerome reads them as "the diversity of gifts," the Gospel doctrine given "to one more, to another less... as meeting the capacity of the receivers" (1 Corinthians 3:2). Hilary allegorises the servants as the believing Jews (five), the Gentiles (two), and the Jews who remain in the Law (one), hiding their talent through envy of the Gentiles' salvation.

The Puritan Henry and the grammatical-historical voices keep the plain frame: the master is Christ "the absolute Owner," the servants Christians (and especially ministers), the talents his rich gifts (Henry valuing a talent of silver at over £350), distributed "to every man according to his several ability" (Ephesians 4:8). "Christ's servants have and receive their all from him," Henry notes, "and our receiving from Christ is in order to our working for him." So the witnesses, whether reading the talents as senses, doctrine, or gifts generally, agree the parable sets us in "a state of work and business," every servant entrusted with something to be traded for his Lord. So the witnesses, whatever sense they assign to the talents, agree that the parable sets every servant in a state of active trust, furnished by his ascended Lord with something to be traded for him, and answerable at his return for the use he has made of it.
15:
"And unto one he gave five talents, to another two, and to another one; to every man according to his several ability." The witnesses fix on the proportioning of gifts and the justice of it. The Alexandrian Origen reads the differing measures as "divers degrees of capacity," noting that "one could not hold the measure of another," yet "he who received but one had received no mean endowment, for one talent of such a master is a great thing." He maps the five to one "able to raise all the meanings of the Scriptures to their more divine significations," the two to one taught "carnal doctrine," the one to "the less strong."

The Puritan Henry expounds the apportioning as the act of "a free Agent, dividing to every man severally as he will," fitting gifts to "men's ability as to mind, body, estate, relation," yet "the ability itself is from him." He draws two notes: "every one had some one talent at least... a soul of our own is the one talent we are every one of us entrusted with"; and "all had not alike, for they had not all alike abilities and opportunities" — "some are cut out for service in one kind, others in another, as the members of the natural body." The grammatical-historical Lightfoot and Barnes supply the value of a talent and the custom; JFB and Robertson keep to the narrative.

The Reformed Calvin and the Wesleyan Wesley read the verse as establishing both the diversity and the justice of God's distribution: no servant is left empty, none is given more than he can use, and each is to be judged by what he received, not by another's measure. So the witnesses agree that the unequal gifts are not unequal favour but unequal trust, proportioned by a wisdom that owes nothing and gives freely, and that fixes the standard of the coming reckoning — for to whom much is given, of him much shall be required, while the servant of one talent is answerable only for the one. So the witnesses agree the unequal gifts are not unequal favour but unequal trust, proportioned by a wisdom that owes nothing and gives freely, and fixing the standard of the coming reckoning, that each be judged by his own measure and none by another's.
16:
"Then he that had received the five talents went and traded with the same, and made them other five talents." The witnesses read the prompt diligence of the good servant as the pattern of a faithful Christian life. The Puritan Henry expounds it most fully: "as soon as ever their master was gone, they immediately applied themselves to their business. Those that have so much work to do as every Christian has, need to set about it quickly, and lose not time." He develops the figure of trade: "a true Christian is a spiritual tradesman," whose trade is "a manufacture trade" (something done upon our own hearts and for others) and "a merchant-trade" (parting with things of less value for things of greater — wisdom's merchandise, Proverbs 3:15; Matthew 13:45).

Henry presses that the servant trades "as a factor with our master's stock": "we have no stock of our own to trade with." The endowments of the mind, the enjoyments of the world, the ordinances of the gospel — "bibles, ministers, sabbaths, sacraments" — "must be improved for the end for which they were instituted; and this is trading with our talents." The doubling shows the success: "he that had five talents soon made them other five."

The grammatical-historical Barnes, Ellicott and Robertson keep to the plain narrative: the servant put the trust to immediate and profitable use, and the increase was proportioned to the capital. So the witnesses make the verse the image of active, prompt, and fruitful stewardship — the servant who, the moment his Lord departs, sets his whole gift to work and brings back gain. As Henry adds, "the hand of the diligent makes rich in graces, and comforts, and treasures of good works; there is a great deal to be got by industry in religion." The five become ten because the servant lost no time and spared no diligence with what was never his own. Gill adds that the prompt trading of the good servant is the very pattern of a living faith, which sets to work the moment it receives, and that the doubling of the talents is the increase of grace that always follows where the gifts of God are diligently improved for him.
17:
"And likewise he that had received two, he also gained other two." The witnesses comment briefly, noting that the servant of two talents is commended on exactly the same footing as the servant of five. The point pressed across this passage is that faithfulness, not the size of the gift, is what the Lord rewards: the man of two doubled his trust as truly as the man of five doubled his, and both will hear the same word of approval.

The Puritan Henry (across these verses) draws the encouragement this offers to those of lesser gifts: "from those to whom he has given but two talents, he expects only the improvement of two, which may encourage those who are placed in a lower and narrower sphere of usefulness; if they lay out themselves to do good according to the best of their capacity and opportunity, they shall be accepted, though they do not so much good as others." The returns, he notes, "were in proportion to the receivings" — five expected of five, two of two.

The brevity of the comment matches the design of the verse, which exists chiefly to establish the parallel between the two faithful servants. As Gill and Barnes note, the servant of two talents traded with the same promptness and fidelity as his fellow, and gained the same proportionate increase. So the witnesses read the verse as quietly central to the parable's doctrine: the Lord does not measure his servants against one another but each against his own trust, and the man who faithfully doubles a smaller stock stands, in the reckoning to come, on equal ground with the man who doubles a larger. What is required of every servant is not greatness but faithfulness with what he has received. Gill and Barnes note that the servant of two traded with the same promptness and fidelity as his fellow and gained the same proportionate increase, so that the verse quietly establishes the equality of the faithful, whatever the size of their trust, before the equal welcome both are about to receive. The brevity of the comment matches the design of the verse, which exists chiefly to establish the parallel between the two faithful servants and to teach that what the Lord requires of every servant is not greatness but faithfulness with what he has received.
18:
"But he that had received one went and digged in the earth, and hid his lord's money." The witnesses read the slothful servant as the type of those who, having received least, do nothing with it at all. The Puritan Henry notes the sobering arithmetic: "though the parable represents but one in three unfaithful, yet in a history that answers this parable we find the disproportion quite the other way" — of ten lepers cleansed, "nine of ten hid the talent, and only one returned to give thanks" (Luke 17:17-18). And he observes that "the unfaithful servant was he that had but one talent," though "doubtless there are many that have five talents and bury them all — great abilities, great advantages, and yet do no good with them."

The grammatical-historical commentators take the hiding of the talent as the picture of the gift unused — not squandered in riot, but simply buried, withheld from the service for which it was given. The fault is not active wickedness but the refusal to trade, the burying of a trust that was meant to be put to work.

The Wesleyan Wesley and Gill keep the plain sense: the one-talent servant, instead of trading, dug in the earth and concealed his lord's money, treating the trust as something to be kept safe and idle rather than employed. So the witnesses make the verse the parable's warning against a sin easily overlooked — not the misuse of gifts but their non-use. As Gregory had taught (on v.14), "to hide one's talent in the earth is to devote the ability we have received to worldly business"; the servant who does no positive evil yet renders no service is, in the parable's reckoning, the one who fails. The buried talent is the gift God gave to be traded, kept instead in the ground of a slothful and unprofitable life. Gill notes that the one-talent servant did no positive evil but simply buried his trust, and that this sin of omission, the gift withheld from service, is the very thing the parable singles out for condemnation, warning that to render Christ nothing is itself to fail him.
19:
"After a long time the lord of those servants cometh, and reckoneth with them." The witnesses dwell on the long delay and the certainty of the reckoning. The Latin Jerome marks the interval: "After a long time, because there is a long interval between the Saviour's ascension and His second coming." The Alexandrian Origen reads the delay as the time during which Christ "has sent forth such as are fitted to bring about the salvation of souls," noting that even the apostles "lived to old age" (John 21:18); and he observes that "the servants do not come to the Lord to be judged, but the Lord shall come to them when the time shall be accomplished."

The Antiochene Chrysostom finds in the deferred reckoning a lesson in the Lord's "long suffering" — "He does not require the reckoning immediately" — and hears in it a covert allusion "to the resurrection," when each servant shall give account. The reckoning is sure, however long postponed.

The grammatical-historical commentators keep the plain sense: the master returns, and every servant must render account of what he did with the trust. So the witnesses read the verse as the talents-parable's counterpart to the bridegroom's midnight coming in the parable of the virgins — the long-delayed but certain arrival that turns the whole story upon the use that was made of the waiting time. The "long time" both tests the servants' fidelity and magnifies the Lord's patience; but it ends, as all such delays end, in a reckoning from which none is exempt. As Gill notes, the coming to reckon is Christ's coming to judgment, when the secrets of every stewardship shall be brought to light and each servant answer for his own. Gill notes that the coming to reckon is Christ's coming to judgment, when the secrets of every stewardship shall be brought to light and each servant answer for his own, so that the long delay both tries the servants' fidelity and magnifies the patience of the Lord who at last returns. The witnesses thus read the verse as the talents-parable's counterpart to the bridegroom's midnight coming, the long-delayed but certain arrival that turns the whole story upon the use made of the waiting time.
20:
"And so he that had received five talents came and brought other five talents, saying, Lord, thou deliveredst unto me five talents: behold, I have gained beside them five talents more." The witnesses read the good servant's report as the model of faithful and humble accounting. He brings the increase, but ascribes the original trust to his Lord — "thou deliveredst unto me five talents" — owning that the capital was never his own.

The Reformed Calvin and the grammatical-historical commentators note that the servant comes "with boldness," as Origen says (on the parallel verse), yet without boasting: he reports the gain plainly as the fruit of the Lord's stock put to work. The doubling is presented not as his achievement but as the return on what was entrusted, the servant standing as a faithful steward who has discharged his trust.

The witnesses, across the schools, make the verse the picture of the account that the diligent will gladly render. Where the slothful servant will come with excuses and complaint, the faithful comes with the increase in hand and a clear conscience, able to say, "behold, I have gained." Ellicott and the expositors observe that the servant's words exactly match his Lord's original entrustment — five received, five gained — so that the account is open, honest, and complete. As Gill notes, the bringing of the talents doubled is the bringing forth of the fruit of grace improved, the works that follow a living faith; and the servant's joy in his accounting is the joy of one who has been about his Master's business and has something to show for the waiting. The verse thus sets the faithful report over against the unfaithful one to come, the gain answered by approval as the excuse will be answered by condemnation. Gill notes that the bringing of the talents doubled is the bringing forth of the fruit of grace improved, the works that follow a living faith, and that the servant's joy in his accounting is the joy of one who has been about his Master's business and has something to show for the waiting.
21:
"Well done, thou good and faithful servant... enter thou into the joy of thy lord." The witnesses dwell on the commendation, the promotion, and above all the "joy." The Latin Raban hears in "Well done" "an interjection of joy," the Lord "shewing the joy with which He invites the servant who labours well to eternal bliss" (Psalm 16:11). The Antiochene Chrysostom takes "thou good servant" of "that goodness which is shewn towards our neighbour," and the "joy" as expressing "complete blessedness."

The Latin Gregory the Great expounds the reward: the faithful servant "is set over many things when, having overcome the afflictions of corruption, he joys with eternal joy in that heavenly seat"; he is "fully admitted to the joy of his Lord when, taken in to that abiding country and numbered among the companies of Angels," he has "such inward joy that there is no room for outward sorrow." Jerome notes that "thou wast faithful in a few things" is said "because all that we have at present, though they seem great and many, yet in comparison of the things to come are little and few"; and "what greater thing can be given to a faithful servant than to be with his Lord, and to see his Lord's joy?"

The grammatical-historical commentators and JFB keep to the structure: the reward is threefold — approval ("well done"), advancement ("ruler over many things"), and admission to the Lord's own joy. Cyril of Jerusalem cites the words as the blessing the faithful long to hear. So the witnesses agree that the highest reward is not a larger trust merely but communion with the Lord himself — to "enter into the joy of thy lord," sharing the gladness of the Master whose business has been faithfully done. As Augustine adds (here), "this will be our perfect joy, than which is none greater, to have fruition of that Divine Trinity in whose image we were made." So the witnesses agree that the highest reward is not a larger trust merely but communion with the Lord himself, to enter into his joy and share the gladness of the Master whose business has been faithfully done, a reward measured by fidelity rather than by the magnitude of the gain.
22:
"He also that had received two talents came and said, Lord, thou deliveredst unto me two talents: behold, I have gained two other talents beside them." The witnesses comment briefly, the verse closely paralleling the report of the five-talent servant, and the brevity is itself instructive. The servant of two talents renders exactly the same kind of account as his fellow: he owns the trust as his Lord's, brings the increase in hand, and presents a doubling proportioned to what he received.

Gill, who carries the comment here, notes that this second servant, like the first, comes with confidence and gladness, having been faithful with the smaller stock entrusted to him. The whole point of the verse, drawn out by the witnesses across this passage, is that the two-talent man stands on precisely the same footing as the five-talent man — he too has traded, he too has gained, and he too will hear the identical commendation in the verse that follows.

So the parable, by repeating the account almost word for word, presses its central lesson: the Lord does not reward according to the magnitude of the gift but according to the fidelity of the servant. The man who doubled two is as welcome as the man who doubled five, for both used what they had. As Jerome had observed (on v.21), the master "looks not to the largeness of their profit, but to the disposition of their will," receiving "with equal favour" the one who of five made ten and the one who of two made four. The verse thus quietly establishes the equality of the faithful, whatever the size of their trust, and prepares for the equal welcome both are about to receive into the joy of their Lord. Gill notes that the second servant comes, like the first, with confidence and gladness, having been faithful with the smaller stock entrusted to him, so that the near-identical report presses the parable's lesson that the Lord rewards the disposition of the will, not the largeness of the profit. The witnesses, by noting how closely this account echoes the first, press the parable's central lesson: that the Lord measures his servants not against one another but each against his own trust, and welcomes the man who doubled two as gladly as the man who doubled five.
23:
"Well done, good and faithful servant; thou hast been faithful over a few things, I will make thee ruler over many things: enter thou into the joy of thy lord." The witnesses note that the commendation of the two-talent servant is word for word the same as that of the five-talent servant, and they make this identity the heart of the verse. The Lord's "Well done" falls equally on both, for both were faithful with what they received.

Gill and Cyril of Jerusalem, who carry the comment here, draw out the equality: the same approval, the same advancement "over many things," and the same admission "into the joy of thy lord" are granted to the servant of two as to the servant of five. The reward is measured not by the amount of the gain but by the faithfulness of the trade; and since both servants were equally faithful, both receive an equal welcome.

So the witnesses read the verse as the parable's clearest statement of its governing principle. Whatever the difference in gifts — five, two, or one — the standard of judgment is the same: faithfulness with what was given. The man of smaller capacity who used it well is set over many things no less than the man of larger; and both alike are called into the Lord's own joy. Cyril of Jerusalem cites the words as the blessing the faithful long to hear, the counterpart to the dread "I know you not" of the foolish virgins. The repetition, far from idle, drives home that the kingdom's rewards are not graded by natural endowment but by the use a servant makes of his trust, so that the humblest faithful steward shares fully in the joy of his Lord. So the repetition, far from idle, drives home that the kingdom's rewards are not graded by natural endowment but by the use a servant makes of his trust, so that the humblest faithful steward shares fully in the joy of his Lord, hearing the very word the man of greater gifts receives.
