24:
"Then he which had received the one talent came and said, Lord, I knew thee that thou art an hard man, reaping where thou hast not sown." The witnesses read the slothful servant's defence as a self-condemning slander against his Lord. The Alexandrian Origen observes that "this servant seems to have been one of those who believe but do not act honestly, concealing their faith"; such men "have a fear of God, and regard Him as austere and implacable." He weighs the charge: "the Lord did not allow that He was a hard man as the servant supposed," yet "He is indeed hard to those who abuse the mercy of God to suffer themselves to become remiss."

The Latin Jerome reads the excuse as adding "to slothfulness and idleness the sin of pride": the servant "who ought to have honestly acknowledged his fault, on the contrary cavils against his Lord, and avers that he did it with provident design, lest while he sought to make profit he should hazard the capital." His pretended caution is in truth a libel on the master's character, turning his own negligence into an accusation against the one he failed to serve.

The Reformed Calvin, the Wesleyan Wesley, and the grammatical-historical commentators keep the plain sense: the servant excuses his inaction by blaming the supposed harshness of his Lord, as though the fault of the buried talent lay with the giver rather than the keeper. So the witnesses make the verse the unmasking of the slothful heart. The man who has done nothing does not confess but accuses, painting God as a grasping master to justify his own idleness — the very pattern, as the witnesses note across this passage, of those who make the strictness of God an excuse for serving him not at all. As Gill observes, the charge is false, and out of his own mouth the servant shall be judged. Gill observes that the charge against the Lord is false, and that out of his own mouth the servant shall be judged, the pretended caution proving in truth a libel on the master's character, by which the idle seek to shift to God the blame for a negligence that was wholly their own.
25:
"And I was afraid, and went and hid thy talent in the earth: lo, there thou hast that is thine." The witnesses read the servant's plea as the confession of a fear that produced not reverence but paralysis. He hid the talent, he says, out of fear — but the fear that should have driven him to diligence instead drove him to bury the trust and do nothing.

The grammatical-historical and Wesleyan commentators note the false safety of his reasoning: he returns the talent intact, "lo, there thou hast that is thine," as though preserving the capital unspent were faithfulness. But the master had not given the talent to be kept; he had given it to be traded. To return it unused is not innocence but failure, the very thing the parable condemns.

Gill, Barnes, Ellicott and Wesley keep the plain sense: the servant's fear is the cover for his sloth, and his boast of having kept the talent safe is in fact the proof of his unprofitableness. So the witnesses make the verse the exposure of a religion that thinks it enough to do no harm. The man who buries his gift and gives it back whole imagines he has discharged his trust, but he has only proved that he never understood it. As Origen had observed (on the surrounding verses), such a servant "uses not" his ability and so loses it; and as Jerome noted, the very excuse he offers becomes his condemnation. The talent returned untraded is the gift God gave to be employed, hidden instead in the earth of a fearful and idle heart, and presented at the reckoning as though its mere preservation were service. Gill and the others note that the servant's boast of having kept the talent safe is in fact the proof of his unprofitableness, since the gift was given to be traded and not buried, so that his fearful preservation of it is precisely the failure the parable condemns. The witnesses thus make the verse the exposure of a religion that thinks it enough to do no harm, when the master had given the talent not to be kept but to be employed, so that the unused gift returned whole is itself the proof of the servant's failure.
26:
"His lord answered and said unto him, Thou wicked and slothful servant, thou knewest that I reap where I sowed not." The witnesses read the master's reply as the turning of the servant's own words against him. The Latin Jerome notes that the Lord "calls him wicked servant because he cavilled against his Lord, and slothful because he would not double his talent, condemning his pride in the one and his idleness in the other." The two charges answer to the two sins the excuse revealed.

The grammatical-historical and Wesleyan commentators observe that the master does not admit the slander but argues from it: granting, for the sake of argument, the servant's own premise, even on that premise his conduct is inexcusable. If he truly believed his Lord to be exacting, that should have spurred him to greater diligence, not lulled him into idleness. The excuse, far from defending him, supplies the ground of his condemnation.

Gill, Barnes, Ellicott and Robertson keep the plain force: "thou wicked and slothful servant" names the double fault — wickedness in the false accusation, sloth in the unused gift. So the witnesses make the verse the master's just sentence upon the man who did nothing and then blamed his Lord for it. As Origen had observed, "the Lord did not allow that He was a hard man, but assented to all his other words" only to expose the servant out of his own mouth. The very plea by which the slothful sought to shield himself becomes the indictment under which he is condemned; for he who knew (or professed to know) his Lord's strictness, and yet would not so much as put the money to the exchangers, stands self-convicted of an idleness for which no fear can excuse him. Gill notes that the master argues from the servant's own premise only to expose him, granting the slander for the sake of argument and showing that even on those terms his idleness is inexcusable, so that the very plea by which he sought to shield himself becomes the indictment under which he falls. The witnesses thus make the verse the master's just sentence on the man who did nothing and then blamed his Lord for it, naming the double fault of wickedness in the false charge and sloth in the unused gift.
27:
"Thou oughtest therefore to have put my money to the exchangers, and then at my coming I should have received mine own with usury." The witnesses read the master's argument as closing every avenue of excuse. The Latin Jerome explains the figure: the money or silver is "the preaching of the Gospel and the heavenly word" (Psalm 12:6), which "ought to be given to the bankers, that is, either to the other doctors... or to all the believers, who can double the sum and restore it with usury by fulfilling in act what they have learned in word." Even if the servant would not trade himself, he might have entrusted the gift to others who would.

The Alexandrian Origen and the grammatical-historical commentators note the force of the reasoning: the master takes the servant on his own terms — "if you knew me to be hard," then you should have at least done the minimum, lending the money at interest, so that it might increase without your labour. The least diligence would have sufficed; the servant rendered not even that. Lightfoot and the expositors note that the "exchangers" or bankers were those who paid interest on deposits, so that the servant's failure was not even excusable as prudence.

JFB, Robertson and Wesley keep the plain sense: there was an easy and safe way to make the talent fruitful, and the servant took not even that. So the witnesses make the verse the final stripping away of the slothful servant's defence. He had pleaded fear and caution; the master shows that fear and caution, if genuine, would themselves have prompted at least the minimal increase of putting the money out at interest. The buried talent had not even the excuse of a careful stewardship; it was sheer idleness, condemned by the very prudence it pretended to. Gill notes that the gift unimproved, by any means whatever, leaves the servant wholly without answer. Gill notes that the gift left wholly unimproved, by any means whatever, leaves the servant entirely without answer, for there was an easy and even profitable way to make the talent fruitful, and the slothful took not even that least and safest course.
28:
"Take therefore the talent from him, and give it unto him which hath ten talents." The witnesses read the master's order as the just removal of an unused gift and its bestowal on the diligent. The grammatical-historical commentators note that the talent is not destroyed but transferred — taken from the one who buried it and given to the one who had proved most faithful, so that the gift passes to where it will be used.

The Latin Gregory the Great (on v.14) had wrestled with why the talent should go "rather to him who had five than to him who had two," and answered from his allegory that the one talent of understanding belongs by right to him "who had administered well the things without"; "the same we see happen every day in the Holy Church, that they who administer faithfully things without are also mighty in the inward understanding." The principle, however the details are read, is that gifts gravitate toward faithfulness.

Ellicott and the expositors keep the plain sense: the unprofitable servant forfeits even the single talent he would not use, and it is added to the abundance of the faithful. So the witnesses make the verse the enactment of a law that the next verse will state in full — that what is not used is lost, and given to those who have used what they had. The buried talent, withheld so carefully from service, is taken away altogether; and the servant who clutched it in the ground loses the very thing he sought to keep. As Gill notes, this is the righteous course of providence and grace, by which the slothful are stripped of their neglected advantages and the diligent enriched, the kingdom's gifts flowing ever toward the hands that will trade with them. Gill notes that this is the righteous course of providence and grace, by which the slothful are stripped of their neglected advantages and the diligent enriched, the kingdom's gifts flowing ever toward the hands that will trade with them, so that the buried talent is lost to the very servant who clutched it.
29:
"For unto every one that hath shall be given, and he shall have abundance: but from him that hath not shall be taken away even that which he hath." The witnesses read this proverbial saying as the law of the kingdom governing the use of all gifts. The Latin Gregory the Great expounds it through charity: "whosoever has charity receives the other gifts also; but whosoever has not charity loses even the gifts which he seemed to have had" — and so "for punishment he shall be cast into outer darkness who has of his own free will fallen into inward darkness."

The Latin Jerome reads it of faith and use: "to him who has faith, and a right will in the Lord, even if he come somewhat short in deed as being man, shall be given by the merciful Judge; but he who has not faith shall lose even the other virtues which he seems to have naturally." He marks the careful wording — "from him that hath not shall be taken even that which he seemeth to have" — for whatever is "without faith in Christ ought not to be imputed to him who uses it amiss, but to Him who gives the goods of nature even to a wicked servant."

The Reformed Calvin, the Wesleyan Wesley, and the grammatical-historical commentators keep the plain principle: gifts used are increased, gifts unused are forfeited. So the witnesses make the verse the parable's general law and the justification of the talent's transfer. The diligent grow richer in grace because grace exercised multiplies; the slothful lose even what they had, because a gift refused is a gift withdrawn. Cyril of Jerusalem cites the saying among the warnings to improve what God bestows. The "having" that matters, the witnesses agree, is not bare possession but faithful use; and by that measure the servant who would not trade is found, in the end, to "have not," and loses the talent he never truly held. So the witnesses agree that the 'having' which matters is not bare possession but faithful use, and that by this measure the servant who would not trade is found in the end to 'have not,' losing the talent he never truly held, while the diligent are enriched because grace exercised always multiplies.
30:
"And cast ye the unprofitable servant into outer darkness: there shall be weeping and gnashing of teeth." The witnesses read the sentence on the slothful servant as the parable's solemn warning that the mere non-use of gifts is itself damning. The Antiochene Chrysostom presses this: "not only he who robs others, or who works evil, is punished with extreme punishment, but he also who does not good works." The servant did no positive wrong; he simply hid his talent — and for that omission he is cast out.

The Alexandrian Origen reads the "outer darkness" as the place "where is no light... and where God is not seen, but those who are condemned thereto are condemned as unworthy the contemplation of God." The punishment is privation as well as pain — exclusion from the light and the presence that the faithful enter as their joy.

The Reformed Calvin, the Wesleyan Wesley, and the grammatical-historical commentators keep the plain force: the unprofitable servant is condemned, and the "weeping and gnashing of teeth" express the anguish of the lost. So the witnesses make the verse the talents-parable's counterpart to the shut door of the virgins — the casting out of those who failed the trust. The lesson they draw is the same Chrysostom and Gregory pressed: that in the kingdom there is no neutral ground, no safe burying of a gift, no innocence in mere inactivity. The servant who renders no service is not acquitted but condemned; the talent withheld is judged as severely as a talent abused. As Gill notes, the doom of the unprofitable is the doom of all who, having received from Christ, render him nothing again — cast from the light into the darkness without, where the loss of God is the deepest of all torments. Gill notes that the doom of the unprofitable is the doom of all who, having received from Christ, render him nothing again, cast from the light into the darkness without, where the loss of God himself is the deepest of all the torments the lost endure. The witnesses thus make the verse the talents-parable's counterpart to the shut door of the virgins, the casting out of those who failed the trust, and the proof that in the kingdom there is no innocence in mere inactivity and no safe burying of a gift.
31:
"When the Son of man shall come in his glory, and all the holy angels with him, then shall he sit upon the throne of his glory." The witnesses agree the parables now give way to a direct description of the last judgment. The Antiochene Chrysostom marks the change: Christ "does not say of this as of the others, The kingdom of heaven is like, but shews of Himself by direct revelation, When the Son of man shall come in his majesty." The angels come "to bear witness to the things wherein they have administered to men's salvation." The Latin Augustine notes that "the wicked and they also who shall be set on His right hand shall see Him in human shape, for He shall appear in the judgment in that form which He took on Him from us"; only afterwards shall he be seen "in the form of God."

The Alexandrian Origen reads the coming "with glory, that His body may be such as when He was transfigured on the mount," and the throne as "certain of the more perfect of the Saints" or "Angelic Powers" (Colossians 1:16). Remigius observes that the words "overthrow the error of those who said that the Lord should not continue in the same form of a servant," for "His majesty" means "His divinity, in which He is equal to the Father." The Latin Raban and Gregory take this as the description of "the manner of the judgment to come," when the King "shall pronounce the sentence."

The Puritan Henry expounds the scene with characteristic fulness: there is a judgment to come, committed to the Son of man (Acts 17:31), who judges "because he is to judge the sons of men" and is recompensed for his condescension by this exaltation; his appearing "will be splendid and glorious" — "the Sun of righteousness shall then shine in his meridian lustre"; he comes "in the glory of his Father" and "in his own glory as mediator," bringing "all his holy angels" as "ministers of his justice," to sit at last "upon the throne of his glory, the throne of judgment" (Daniel 7:9). So the witnesses agree this is no parable but the unveiling of the reality the parables foreshadowed — the glorious return of the once-lowly Son of man to judge the world.
32:
"And before him shall be gathered all nations: and he shall separate them one from another, as a shepherd divideth his sheep from the goats." The witnesses dwell on the universality of the gathering and the exactness of the separation. The Puritan Henry expounds the first: "the judgment of the great day will be a general judgment. All must be summoned before Christ's tribunal; all of every age of the world, from the beginning to the end of time; all of every place on earth." Gill, however, notes that the part here described, with the foregoing parables, "only concerns" those who have professed the Christian religion, giving "a general and lively idea of the whole."

On the separation, Henry draws out its precision: "wicked and godly here dwell together... and are not certainly distinguishable; but in that day they will be separated, and parted for ever" (Malachi 3:18). "This separation will be so exact that the most inconsiderable saint shall not be lost in the crowd of sinners, nor the most plausible sinner hid in the crowd of saints" (Psalm 1:5). The image is drawn from Ezekiel 34:17, "I judge between cattle and cattle"; Christ "is the great Shepherd," who "will shortly distinguish between those that are his and those that are not."

The grammatical-historical commentators and the Reformed Calvin note that the godly are likened to sheep — "innocent, mild, patient, useful" — and the wicked to goats, "a baser kind of animal, unsavoury and unruly," the two now "feeding all day in the same pasture, but coted at night in different folds." Cyril of Jerusalem joins the verse to the certainty of the resurrection and judgment. So the witnesses make the verse the great sorting of mankind: every distinction of rank, learning, and nation abolished, and one division alone remaining — not rich and poor, but righteous and wicked, sheep set on the right hand and goats on the left, by the unerring discernment of the Shepherd who knows his own. The witnesses thus make the verse the great sorting of mankind, in which every distinction of rank, learning, and nation is abolished, and one division alone remains — not rich and poor, but righteous and wicked, by the unerring discernment of the Shepherd who knows his own.
33:
"And he shall set the sheep on his right hand, but the goats on the left." The witnesses read the placing as the public assignment of honour and shame. The Puritan Henry draws out the significance of the hands: "Christ puts honour upon the godly, as we show respect to those we set on our right hand; but the wicked shall rise to everlasting shame" (Daniel 12:2). He stresses what the division is and is not: "it is not said that he shall put the rich on his right hand, and the poor on his left; the learned and noble on his right hand, and the unlearned on his left; but the godly on his right hand, and the wicked on his left."

Henry presses the abolition of every other distinction: "all other divisions and subdivisions will then be abolished; but the great distinction of men into saints and sinners, sanctified and unsanctified, will remain for ever, and men's eternal state will be determined by it." The wicked, he adds, "took up with left-handed blessings, riches and honour, and so shall their doom be."

The Alexandrian Origen (on the surrounding verses) reads the right hand as "rest and glory" and the left as "the misery of torments," and the names themselves as moral: the saved are sheep "by reason of that mildness which they have learnt of Him who said, Learn of me, for I am meek" (Matthew 11:29), ready "to go even to death in imitation of Christ" (Isaiah 53:7); the wicked are goats "because they climb rough and rugged rocks, and walk in dangerous places." The Gloss notes that "under the figure of a sheep is signified simplicity and innocence." So the witnesses make the verse the moment when the inward difference of character becomes an outward and eternal separation — the meek and faithful set in the place of honour, the unruly and unfaithful in the place of shame, by a division that no earthly rank can alter and no future age will undo. The witnesses thus make the verse the moment when the inward difference of character becomes an outward and eternal separation, the meek and faithful set in the place of honour and the unruly and unfaithful in the place of shame, by a division no earthly rank can alter.
34:
"Then shall the King say unto them on his right hand, Come, ye blessed of my Father, inherit the kingdom prepared for you from the foundation of the world." The witnesses dwell on every word of this welcome. The Antiochene Chrysostom notes the verb: "He says not Receive, but Possess, or Inherit, as due to you from of old." The Latin Augustine distinguishes this kingdom from the present Church, which is also called his kingdom, "though in a very inferior manner," where "we are yet in conflict with the enemy until we come to that kingdom of peace, where we shall reign without an enemy."

The Puritan Henry expounds the sentence as the saints' glory. The Shepherd is now "the King, which bespeaks the authority wherewith he will pronounce the sentence." He "pronounces them blessed, and his saying they are blessed makes them so"; "blessed of his Father — reproached and cursed by the world, but blessed of God" (Ephesians 1:3). And the "Come" is "in effect, Welcome, ten thousand welcomes... come to be for ever with me; you that followed me bearing the cross, now come along with me wearing the crown." On "prepared from the foundation of the world," the Latin Jerome notes it is "to be understood of the foreknowledge of God, with whom things to come are as already done."

The grammatical-historical commentators and the Reformed Calvin keep the doctrine: the kingdom is an inheritance, not a wage — a thing "prepared," given of the Father's free grace, and entered by those whose faith has borne the fruit the following verses describe. Cyril of Jerusalem cites the welcome as the blessing the faithful long to hear. So the witnesses agree that the saints' reward is no afterthought but an eternal purpose of love, an inheritance fitted for them "from the foundation of the world," into which the King now calls his own with a welcome that crowns the cross they bore for him. The witnesses thus agree that the saints' reward is no afterthought but an eternal purpose of love, an inheritance fitted for them from the foundation of the world, into which the King now calls his own with a welcome that crowns the cross they bore for him.
35:
"For I was an hungred, and ye gave me meat: I was thirsty, and ye gave me drink: I was a stranger, and ye took me in." The witnesses read the works of mercy as the evidence on which the righteous are owned, and they probe the mystery that Christ receives them as done to himself. The Antiochene Chrysostom marvels at the lightness of what is required: "look how light things He enjoins" — not costly dainties but "necessary food," not deliverance from prison but a visit. He counts seven aggravations of the failure to show such mercy, "for it is God who receives in the poor man," and "what He takes from us is our own."

The Alexandrian Origen explains how the impassible Christ can hunger: "it is written to the believers, Ye are the body of Christ" (1 Corinthians 12:27); "as the soul, though it hungers not in its spiritual substance, yet hungers for the food of the body because yoked to it, so the Saviour suffers whatever His body the Church suffers, though He Himself be impassible." He notes, too, that "in speaking to the righteous He reckons up their good deeds under their several kinds," dwelling on each, "for it was the part of a merciful Judge to enlarge upon men's good deeds."

The grammatical-historical, Reformed and Wesleyan commentators keep the plain doctrine: the works named are not the meritorious cause of salvation but its evidence and fruit, the genuine marks of a living faith and love. Cyril of Jerusalem and the expositors note that the six works of mercy are the test by which the King distinguishes his own. So the witnesses agree that the verse exalts the humblest acts of charity to an astonishing dignity: in feeding the hungry and sheltering the stranger, the righteous, often unawares, ministered to Christ himself, who counts the mercy shown to the least of his people as shown to him. As Gill notes, these are the fruits of grace, not its price, and they are produced in the saints by the same Spirit that unites them to their Lord.
36:
"Naked, and ye clothed me: I was sick, and ye visited me: I was in prison, and ye came unto me." The witnesses continue the catalogue of mercies, and they note again the ease and ordinariness of the deeds the King commends. The works named require not wealth or station but compassion: clothing the naked, visiting the sick, coming to the imprisoned — acts within the reach of the humblest believer.

The grammatical-historical commentators observe that these complete the six works of mercy by which, as Remigius noted (on v.31), "whoso shall study to accomplish them shall be entitled to the kingdom." The list is not exhaustive but representative, naming the plain duties of love toward the suffering, and standing for the whole habit of mercy that marks the children of the kingdom.

The witnesses, across the schools, stress that what is rewarded is not a single heroic act but a settled disposition of charity, shown in the common occasions of human need. Chrysostom had observed (on v.35) the lightness of the things enjoined; here too the King points not to extraordinary feats but to the ready kindness that clothes, visits, and comes near. Cyril of Jerusalem and the expositors read the verse as the continued evidence of a living faith, which expresses itself not in profession only but in active love toward Christ's afflicted members. So the verse, with those before it, makes the works of mercy the visible token of the inward grace that the parables of the virgins and the talents had pressed under other figures — the oil in the vessel, the talent traded, now appearing as the clothing of the naked and the visiting of the sick, the fruit by which the righteous are known and owned. As Gill notes, these deeds, done to the least, are accepted by the King as done to himself. The witnesses thus make the works of mercy the visible token of the inward grace that the parables of the virgins and the talents had pressed under other figures, the oil in the vessel and the talent traded now appearing as the clothing of the naked and the visiting of the sick.
37:
"Then shall the righteous answer him, saying, Lord, when saw we thee an hungred, and fed thee?" The witnesses dwell on the humble surprise of the righteous, who do not recognise their own works as the King recounts them. The Latin Raban explains that "they say this not because they distrust the Lord's words, but they are in amaze at so great exaltation, and at the greatness of their own glory; or because the good which they have done will seem to them to be so small" beside the reward (Romans 8:18).

The Alexandrian Origen reads their answer as the mark of true humility: "it is from humility that they declare themselves unworthy of any praise for their good deeds, not that they are forgetful of what they have done." And he notes the contrast with the wicked: "the righteous dwell upon each word, while the unrighteous answer summarily," for "it becomes the righteous out of humility to disclaim each individual generous action when imputed to them publicly."

The grammatical-historical, Reformed and Wesleyan commentators keep the same sense: the righteous are genuinely astonished, having served Christ in his poor without calculating reward or even perceiving that it was Christ they served. So the witnesses make the verse a window into the character of true charity. The works that justify the righteous before the world's eye were done without self-regard, so unconsciously that the doers cannot recall them as the King names them. Their question, "when saw we thee?", is not denial but wonder — the wonder of those who loved the least for the King's sake without measuring their love, and now find it counted as love to the King himself. As Gill notes, the surprise of the righteous proves that their works flowed from grace and not from a desire of recompense, the very disinterestedness that marks them as truly his. The witnesses thus make the verse a window into the character of true charity, whose works were done so unconsciously and so free from self-regard that the doers cannot recall them as the King names them, their very wonder proving that their love flowed from grace and not from a desire of reward.
38:
"When saw we thee a stranger, and took thee in? or naked, and clothed thee?" The witnesses comment briefly, the verse continuing the wondering question of the righteous begun in the verse before. Gill and Barnes note that the righteous rehearse the King's own list back to him as a question, unable to recall the occasions on which they ministered to him, because they had served his poor without thought of serving him.

The point pressed across this passage is the genuine unconsciousness of true charity. The righteous do not say, as the self-righteous might, "we remember these deeds and claim their reward"; they ask, in amazement, when such things were done. Their good works were so natural an outflow of grace, and so free from self-regard, that they passed almost unnoticed by the doers themselves.

So the witnesses read the verse, with those around it, as the portrait of a love that does not keep accounts. The taking in of the stranger and the clothing of the naked were done for the need's sake and for the King's, not for a recompense reckoned and remembered; and now, when the King reveals that he himself was the object of every such mercy, the righteous can only wonder. Their question is the sincere astonishment of humility, which never imagined that its small kindnesses to the lowly were kindnesses to the Lord of glory. As the witnesses note, this disclaiming of their own deeds is itself part of their commendation, the very disinterestedness that distinguishes the works of grace from the works of pride, and proves that the righteous loved not for reward but for love's sake and their Lord's. The witnesses thus read the verse, with those around it, as the portrait of a love that does not keep accounts. The taking in of the stranger and the clothing of the naked were done for the need's sake and for the King's, not for a recompense reckoned and remembered; and now, when the King reveals that he himself was the object of every such mercy, the righteous can only wonder, their disclaiming of their own deeds being itself a part of their commendation.
39:
"Or when saw we thee sick, or in prison, and came unto thee?" The witnesses note that the righteous complete their wondering question by naming the last of the works the King had recounted, still unable to recall when they ministered these mercies to him. The verse closes the answer of the righteous, who, having heard the whole catalogue of their good deeds, can only ask in amazement when such service was rendered to their Lord.

Gill and Barnes, who carry the comment here, observe that the righteous pass in review the very acts the King has named — the visiting of the sick, the coming to the imprisoned — and find in their own memory no record of having done them to him. The reason, drawn out across this passage, is that they did them to his poor and afflicted members, without perceiving that in serving the least they served the King himself.

So the verse seals the portrait of the righteous as those who loved without self-regard. The whole of their answer — from "when saw we thee an hungred" to "when saw we thee sick, or in prison" — is one sustained expression of humble surprise, the wonder of souls whose charity was so unforced and so free from calculation that they cannot now claim it as merit. They came to the sick and the prisoner because love moved them, not because they reckoned on reward; and the discovery that Christ counts every such visit as a visit to himself fills them with astonishment rather than self-congratulation. As the witnesses note, this very unawareness is the seal of the genuineness of their works, distinguishing the children of the kingdom, whose good deeds flow unbidden from grace, from the hypocrite who does all to be seen and remembered. The witnesses thus seal the portrait of the righteous as those who loved without self-regard, whose whole answer is one sustained expression of humble surprise, and whose very unawareness of their deeds is the seal of their genuineness, distinguishing the children of the kingdom from the hypocrite who does all to be seen.
40:
"And the King shall answer and say unto them, Verily I say unto you, Inasmuch as ye have done it unto one of the least of these my brethren, ye have done it unto me." The witnesses treat this as the parable's golden saying, and they debate who "the least of these my brethren" are. The Latin Jerome restricts the phrase: "It were free to us to understand that it is Christ in every poor man whom we feed... but when He says, In that ye have done it to one of the least of these my brethren, He seems to me not to speak of the poor generally, but of the poor in spirit," those of whom Christ said, "Whosoever shall do the will of my Father, the same is my brother" (Matthew 12:50).

The Antiochene Chrysostom reads more broadly: "if they are His brethren, why does He call them the least? Because they are lowly, poor, and outcast. By these He means not only the monks who have retired to the mountains, but every believer though he should be secular," for "baptism and the communication of the Divine mysteries makes him a brother." The Alexandrian Origen adds a note of degree: "a deed of mercy shewn to the more holy is more acceptable to God than one shewn to the less holy," for "they are truly His brethren who are perfect."

The grammatical-historical, Reformed and Wesleyan commentators and Cyril of Jerusalem keep the central wonder: Christ so identifies himself with his suffering people that mercy shown to them is mercy shown to him. So the witnesses leave a genuine difference — whether "the least" are the poor at large, or specifically Christ's needy disciples and "brethren" in the faith — while agreeing on the astonishing union the saying reveals: the King receives in his lowliest members the kindness done to them, and counts it as done to his own person, so that no act of charity to a believer is lost or unobserved, but treasured up as a personal gift to Christ himself. The witnesses thus make the saying the astonishing revelation of the King's union with his people, by which no act of charity to a believer is lost or unobserved, but treasured up as a personal gift to Christ himself who receives in his lowliest members the kindness done to them.
41:
"Then shall he say also unto them on the left hand, Depart from me, ye cursed, into everlasting fire, prepared for the devil and his angels." The witnesses weigh the dreadful sentence and note a careful difference in its wording. The grammatical-historical Barnes observes "a remarkable difference between the manner in which the righteous shall be addressed and the wicked": to the one Christ says the kingdom was "prepared for you"; to the other, that the fire was "prepared for the devil," not for them. "They will inherit it because they have the same character as the devil, and therefore are fitted to the same place — not because it was originally fitted for them." Ellicott marks the omission of "of my Father": "He is not the author of the curse"; those who have brought the curse on themselves "He no longer acknowledges as His."

The Wesleyan Clarke draws the same lesson: the place of torment "was then prepared" for the devil and his angels when they sinned, and "never was designed for human souls"; but "as the wicked are partakers with the devil in their iniquities, so it is right that they should be sharers in their punishment." He insists they are "destroyed, not because there was no salvation for them, but because they neglected to receive good and do good... they are cursed because they refused to be blessed, and they are damned because they refused to be saved."

The Reformed Calvin reads the threat as given for the warning of believers, "that they may not envy the present lot" of the reprobate: "as promises are necessary to excite us to holiness, so threatenings are necessary to restrain us by fear." Barnes treats the "fire" as an image of "the certainty and intensity" of the suffering rather than a precise description of its mode. So the witnesses agree the verse reveals both the justice and the self-incurred ruin of the lost: the fire was never prepared for man, and none enters it but by choosing the devil's character and refusing the blessing freely offered. The witnesses thus agree that the verse reveals both the justice and the self-incurred ruin of the lost, for the fire was never prepared for man, and none enters it but by choosing the devil's character and refusing the blessing that was freely offered.
42:
"For I was an hungred, and ye gave me no meat: I was thirsty, and ye gave me no drink." The witnesses read the indictment of the wicked as the exact counterpart of the commendation of the righteous: they are condemned not for great crimes but for the omission of mercy. The same works that proved the righteous are here named in the negative as the proof of the wicked — the hungry unfed, the thirsty given no drink.

The Alexandrian Origen (on v.41-45) had noted that "in speaking to the righteous He reckons up their good deeds under their several kinds, but to the unrighteous He cuts short the description," because "it was the part of a merciful Judge to enlarge upon men's good deeds, but to pass lightly over their evil deeds." The very brevity with which the charge is pressed is itself a mark of the Judge's mercy.

The grammatical-historical commentators and Cyril of Jerusalem keep the central lesson, drawn out by Chrysostom on the parallel verses: that the wicked are condemned for sins of omission, for failing to do the good that lay within their easy reach. They are not charged with robbery or violence but with the simple withholding of bread and water from the needy. So the witnesses make the verse the solemn complement to the parable's blessing. As mercy shown to the least was mercy to Christ, so mercy withheld from the least was mercy withheld from Christ himself; and the failure to feed and give drink, slight as each instance may seem, is here shown to be a failure toward the King, sufficient to fix the eternal portion of those who, having the means and the occasion, would not stretch out a hand to the suffering members of his body. As Gill notes, the omission of these plain duties is the evidence of a heart estranged from Christ. The witnesses thus make the verse the solemn complement to the parable's blessing, for as mercy shown to the least was mercy to Christ, so mercy withheld from the least was mercy withheld from Christ himself, the failure to feed and give drink shown to be a failure toward the King.
43:
"I was a stranger, and ye took me not in: naked, and ye clothed me not: sick, and in prison, and ye visited me not." The witnesses continue the indictment of the wicked, noting that the charge against them is simply the absence of the mercies that marked the righteous. Where the blessed took in the stranger and clothed the naked, the cursed did neither; the same catalogue that crowned the one company condemns the other.

The Alexandrian Origen (on these verses) had observed that to the wicked the Judge "cuts short the description under the one head," and even, in the parallel, gathers their failures together rather than dwelling on each — for "it becomes the righteous out of humility to disclaim each individual generous action, whereas bad men excuse their sins, and endeavour to prove them few and venial." The summary form of the charge answers to the summary excuse the wicked will offer.

Gill, Clarke and Cyril of Jerusalem keep the plain and weighty sense: the wicked are condemned for what they did not do, the good left undone toward Christ's afflicted members. So the witnesses make the verse, with the one before it, the full statement of the negative test. The works of mercy are not an arbitrary standard but the natural fruit of a heart joined to Christ; their total absence betrays a heart that never knew him. The stranger not taken in, the naked not clothed, the sick and imprisoned not visited — these omissions, each small in itself, together compose the evidence on which the King disowns those on his left hand. As the witnesses note, the wicked are not charged with hating Christ's people openly, but with the colder sin of indifference, the failure of a love they never had toward the Lord they professed but did not serve. The witnesses thus make the verse, with the one before it, the full statement of the negative test, in which the wicked are charged not with open hatred of Christ's people but with the colder sin of indifference, the failure of a love they never had toward the Lord they professed but did not serve.
44:
"Then shall they also answer him, saying, Lord, when saw we thee an hungred, or athirst, or a stranger, or naked, or sick, or in prison, and did not minister unto thee?" The witnesses note that the wicked, like the righteous, are surprised — but their surprise is of a wholly different kind. Where the righteous wondered in humility that their small kindnesses were counted as service to Christ, the wicked plead in self-justification that they never knowingly refused him.

The grammatical-historical and Reformed commentators observe the telltale brevity of their answer. As Origen had noted on the surrounding verses, the wicked "answer summarily, and not going through the particular instances," for "bad men excuse their sins, and endeavour to prove them few and venial," whereas the righteous dwell on each word out of humility. The very form of the wicked's reply betrays the self-excusing heart.

The Wesleyan Wesley, Calvin and Gill keep the plain sense: the wicked protest that, had they seen Christ himself in need, they would surely have ministered to him — not perceiving that in neglecting his poor they neglected him. Their plea is in truth their condemnation, for it reveals that they would have served a Christ who could advance them, while despising the lowly members in whom he was truly present. So the witnesses make the verse the mirror-image of the righteous' question. Both companies ask, "when saw we thee?"; but the one asks in wonder that love was rewarded, the other in protest that indifference was punished. The wicked, who would have honoured a visible King, failed the King hidden in his suffering people, and their excuse only proves how little they knew or loved him. As Gill notes, their answer is the answer of those who served self, not Christ, and now find that the service they withheld from the least was withheld from the Lord of all. The witnesses thus make the verse the mirror-image of the righteous' question: both companies ask 'when saw we thee?', but the one in wonder that love was rewarded, the other in protest that indifference was punished, the wicked's very excuse proving how little they knew or loved the King hidden in his suffering people.
45:
"Then shall he answer them, saying, Verily I say unto you, Inasmuch as ye did it not to one of the least of these, ye did it not to me." The witnesses read this as the King's final answer to the wicked, the exact negative of the saying that justified the righteous. As mercy shown to the least was shown to Christ, so mercy withheld from the least was withheld from Christ himself.

The Alexandrian Origen (on these verses) notes a subtle difference in the wording: "to the righteous He says, In that ye did it to my brethren, to shew the greatness of their good deeds; to the sinners He says only, to one of the least of these, not aggravating their sin." Even in condemnation the Judge speaks with a certain restraint, naming the wicked's omission without enlarging upon it, as a merciful Judge passing lightly over evil.

Gill, Barnes and Cyril of Jerusalem keep the central truth: Christ so unites himself to his needy people that what is denied them is denied to him. So the witnesses make the verse the solemn seal of the negative judgment. The whole indictment of the wicked rests on this identification: their cold neglect of the hungry, the stranger, and the sick was, though they knew it not, a neglect of the King in person. The plea "when saw we thee?" is answered by the revelation that he was there all along, in the least of his brethren whom they passed by. And as the saving union with Christ made the righteous' smallest kindness a service to him, so the want of that union left the wicked's whole life barren of the mercy that would have owned him. As Gill notes, the same word that crowns the charity of the blessed condemns the indifference of the cursed, and both turn on the King's nearness to the least of those who are his. The witnesses thus make the verse the solemn seal of the negative judgment, for the whole indictment of the wicked rests on this identification, that their cold neglect of the hungry, the stranger, and the sick was, though they knew it not, a neglect of the King in person.
46:
"And these shall go away into everlasting punishment: but the righteous into life eternal." The witnesses read this closing verse as the deliberate sealing of the doctrine of eternal retribution, and the great patristic debate over it gathers here. The Latin Augustine takes the verse as Christ's express guard against evasion: "some deceive themselves, saying that the fire indeed is called everlasting, but not the punishment. This the Lord foreseeing, sums up His sentence in these words" — the same word "everlasting" qualifying both the punishment of the wicked and the life of the righteous, so that "if both are eternal, then surely either both are to be taken as long-continued but at last ending, or both as endless." He answers the objection that endless pain is unjust for temporal sin: human law does not measure punishment by the time taken to commit the crime, and "the justice of no law is concerned that the duration of the punishment be the same with the sin."

Augustine then names and refutes the milder views. "Among these impugners of the eternity of punishment, Origen is the most merciful, who believed that the Devil himself and his Angels, after sufferings proportioned to their deserts, should be delivered from those torments, and associated with the holy Angels; but for this he was not undeservedly rebuked by the Church." He likewise refutes those who would limit punishment to a time, or promise final liberation to all the baptized, or to Catholics however they live, citing against them the apostolic word that the workers of the flesh "shall not inherit the kingdom of God" (Galatians 5:21).

The grammatical-historical commentators and Cyril of Jerusalem keep the plain parallelism: the same adjective binds the two destinies, so that the everlastingness of the one is the measure of the other. So the witnesses, with Augustine leading and Origen the named dissenter, make the verse the church's classic proof-text for the endlessness of both states. The judgment of the great day issues in two eternal and opposite ends — the punishment of the cursed and the life of the blessed — and the symmetry of the wording forbids us to soften the one while holding fast the other. As Jerome and the expositors note, the verse closes the whole discourse on the last things with a finality that admits no appeal: the door once shut, the talent once removed, the sentence once passed, stand for ever.
